text: Total cessation of training leads to a decrease in VO2max due to reductions in both blood and plasma volume in endurance athletes.
Detraining causes cardiac changes including decreases in left ventricular mass, size, and thickness, along with increased heart rate and blood pressure, resulting in reduced cardiac output.
Detraining leads to declines in lactate threshold and muscle glycogen stores, increased body weight, and altered respiratory exchange ratio in endurance athletes.
Short-term detraining decreases insulin sensitivity in endurance athletes, while glucagon, growth hormone, and cortisol levels remain unchanged.
Detraining causes reductions in arterial-venous oxygen difference and glucose transporter-4 (GLUT-4) in skeletal muscle.
Implementing a partial reduction in training volume rather than total cessation helps mitigate losses in physiological and performance parameters during transition periods between training seasons.
